Headline: Social Networks
Text: From dial-up bulletin boards to the platform giants — and to the states that learned to switch them off. Where social networks rose, were bought, and were banned. A story by MICRI group 1.

Headline: The Rise of Bulletin Board Systems
Text: Toward the end of the 1970s, a group of enthusiasts created Bulletin Board Systems (BBS), rudimentary platforms that users could connect to via modem to leave messages, share files, and participate in discussions. The Computer Bulletin Board System (CBBS), developed in 1978 at the University of Chicago, allowed users to store and transmit messages and is considered one of the earliest examples of a social network.

Headline: Usenet and the First Online Discussions
Text: Usenet was founded in the United States in 1979 by two students at Duke University. It consists of thousands of interconnected servers, each of which collects articles, news, messages, or posts that people with access to the network submit within a given hierarchy, in a publicly accessible archive. Before the advent of the World Wide Web, it allowed users to communicate with one another through newsletters and discussion groups.
Media Caption: A 2004 discussion in the Usenet group

Headline: SixDegrees, the first true social network
Text: In 1997, SixDegrees.com—the first modern social network—was launched. It allowed users to create a personal profile, add friends, and browse their connections, introducing the key features of today’s social networks.

Headline: From Resume to Network: The Beginnings of LinkedIn
Text: LinkedIn was founded in California with the goal of transforming professional relationships into a digital network. It quickly became an essential tool for networking and job searching.
Media Caption: Former LinkedIn headquarters in Stierlin Court in Mountain View, California

Headline: Facebook: From Harvard Dorms to Worldwide Phenomenon
Text: TheFacebook is launched by Mark Zuckerberg and other Harvard students. Initially reserved for the university's students, it expands rapidly to become the most widely used social network in the world.

Headline: The Idea of Three Guys That Changed the Internet
Text: YouTube is born as a project by three former PayPal employees: Chad Hurley, Steve Chen, and Jawed Karim. After abandoning the initial idea of a dating site, it becomes the world's leading platform for video sharing.
Media Caption: From left to right: Chad Hurley, Steve Chen, and Jawed Karim, the founders of YouTube

Headline: Twitter and the Microblogging Revolution
Text: Jack Dorsey publishes the first tweet in history. Twitter introduces microblogging, hashtags, and retweets, becoming one of the main tools for the spread of news in real time.
Media Caption: The Twitter logo, which derives from the English verb "to tweet," meaning to chirp

Headline: Iran Shuts Down the Net
Text: During the protests following the presidential elections, the Iranian government blocks Facebook, Twitter, and other online services to limit the spread of information. This is one of the first major cases of systematic censorship of social networks.
Media Caption: The internet opens a window to the world

Headline: The Birth of Pinterest: The Social Network That Transformed Ideas into Images
Text: Pinterest is launched by Ben Silbermann, Paul Sciarra, and Evan Sharp as a platform to collect and organize inspirational images through digital boards.

Headline: Social Networks as Drivers of Revolution
Text: Instagram is born as a platform dedicated to photography. During the same period, Facebook and Twitter take on a central role during the Arab Spring, facilitating the organization, communication, and spread of the protests.
Media Caption: From hashtags to the streets: social media fueled the Arab Spring.
Source: Wikipedia — Social media's role in the Arab Spring

Headline: Google+: The Elegant Idea That Challenged Facebook
Text: Google launches Google+ with the goal of competing with Facebook by introducing "Circles" to manage social relationships. Despite significant initial interest, the platform fails to build an active community and will close in 2019.

Headline: TikTok and the Short-Video Boom
Text: ByteDance launches Douyin, the Chinese version of TikTok, ushering in a new generation of social media based on short videos and recommendation algorithms.
Media Caption: Douyin ushered in short-video, algorithm-driven social media platforms.

Headline: Goodbye WhatsApp in China: The Great Firewall Grows Even Stronger
Text: The Chinese government progressively makes WhatsApp unusable, strengthening the Great Firewall and favoring domestic platforms like WeChat.

Headline: ByteDance Acquires Musical.ly: TikTok Is Born
Text: ByteDance acquires Musical.ly and integrates it into TikTok, giving rise to the platform that would conquer the global short-video market.

Headline: The Cambridge Analytica Scandal: When Your Data Becomes a Political Weapon
Text: The Cambridge Analytica scandal reveals the improper use of data from millions of Facebook users for political microtargeting campaigns, sparking a massive debate on digital privacy.
Media Caption: Alexander Nix, CEO of Cambridge Analytica, protagonist of the third day of the 2017 Web Summit

Headline: 2020: The Year That Forever Transformed Our Relationship with the Digital World
Text: During the COVID-19 pandemic, social networks experienced a historic surge in usage. They became essential tools for communication, studying, working, and staying informed, but the problem of misinformation also grew.
Media Caption: Map of the spread of COVID-19

Headline: Myanmar: The Facebook Block Signals the Military Regime's Crackdown
Text: Following the coup, the military junta blocks Facebook to hinder the coordination of protests and limit the spread of information.
Media Caption: Myanmar’s struggle becomes a global call for freedom

Headline: The Facebook Group Changes Its Name and Becomes Meta
Text: Facebook Inc. changes its name to Meta, announcing a strategy oriented towards the development of the metaverse and immersive digital experiences.
Media Caption: From a social network to a parallel reality

Headline: Russia Blocks Facebook and Instagram After the Invasion of Ukraine
Text: Following the invasion of Ukraine, Russia has blocked Facebook and Instagram, accusing Meta of extremism and seeking to control the narrative of the conflict.

Headline: Twitter Goes to Elon Musk
Text:
With a lightning-fast tweet reading “The bird is freed,” Elon Musk formalized the purchase of Twitter for $44 billion.
While the American and European far right rejoiced at the change in course and free speech advocates celebrated the event, critics immediately raised concerns: according to many analysts, loosening the reins of control risked opening the floodgates to an uncontrollable wave of political disinformation and fake news.
